text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 21 September 2025 

Dr Sarah Hamilton 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
St Mark’s Hospital, W1N 4AB  

Dear Dr Sarah Hamilton, 

Mr Daniel Kerr, DOB: 22-08-1989, Hospital No: K847205 NHS No. 1039485726  
Flat 3, Greenways Assisted Living, 12 Brook Lane, Reading, RG1 6PT  

Diagnosis: 

Recurrent seizures with uncertain classification; current control suboptimal despite polytherapy 

Aetiology: 

No definitive structural, genetic, or metabolic cause identified to date; prior MRI and EEG unremarkable (reports from 2022 and 2023 respectively). Further characterisation pending correlation with future documented events. 

Other Medical Problems: 

Mild cognitive impairment under community neuropsychology; generalised anxiety symptoms managed conservatively; vitamin D deficiency on supplementation. Supported by a key worker in assisted living; key-worker notes reviewed where available. 

Management Plan: 

1. Continue current regimen: Sodium Valproate 600 mg mane and 800 mg nocte; Clobazam 10 mg nocte; Lacosamide 100 mg mane and 150 mg nocte. Emphasised adherence and single-brand supply where possible.
2. Midazolam 10 mg buccal for any convulsive seizure exceeding five minutes; key worker remains trained in its administration. No recent administration reported.
3. Arrange 7-day ambulatory EEG with event button and concurrent carer diary; will coordinate scheduling with assisted living team.
4. Bloods today: FBC, U&E, LFTs, vitamin D, and trough valproate and lacosamide levels.
5. Falls and bone health: continue vitamin D3 1000 IU daily; refer to physiotherapy for balance assessment and to occupational therapy for environmental review (bathroom rails and bed guard suggested).
6. Safety: reinforced SUDEP risk mitigation—night-time supervision plan unchanged; key worker to continue nocturnal checks and ensure prompt recovery positioning post-event.
7. DVLA: not driving and understands ongoing restrictions.
8. Review in three months, sooner if escalation or injury. We will contact the residence with results and adjust therapy if indicated.

Since the last visit, he remains in supported accommodation. The key worker’s daily logs and incident forms were reviewed. Seizure frequency remains unchanged over the last six months; he continues to have up to three generalised tonic-clonic seizures per week, rarely achieving more than ten consecutive seizure-free days. He also has daily drop attacks. Furthermore, focal impaired-awareness seizures with disorientation are reported every four to six weeks. No events have occurred since his most recent review. There have been two minor soft-tissue injuries due to falls in the bathroom over the past quarter; no head injury or hospital admission. Sleep routine is variable but improving with staff prompts. No clear medication side effects beyond intermittent fatigue and mild tremor, which he finds tolerable. Appetite and weight are stable. 

Previous treatments included Levetiracetam (mood irritability and no efficacy), Topiramate (word-finding difficulty), and Lamotrigine (rash). Current regimen provides partial benefit with reduced post-ictal confusion duration, per carer report. 

I will write again once investigations are complete or sooner if there is any significant change.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequency statements: (1) “up to three generalised tonic-clonic seizures per week” → 3 per week. (2) “He also has daily drop attacks.” → daily = 1 or more per day; exact count not given, but clearly at least once daily. (3) “focal impaired-awareness seizures … every four to six weeks” → 1 per 4 to 6 weeks (less frequent). Apply rule to select the highest frequency among multiple seizure types: daily drop attacks are more frequent than weekly GTCs and 4–6 weekly focal seizures. Therefore final label corresponds to daily. Since the exact number is unspecified but clearly daily, choose “1 per day” as the normalized minimal daily frequency.
seizure_frequency_number: He also has daily drop attacks.
reference: Seizure frequency remains unchanged over the last six months; he continues to have up to three generalised tonic-clonic seizures per week, rarely achieving more than ten consecutive seizure-free days. He also has daily drop attacks. Furthermore, focal impaired-awareness seizures with disorientation are reported every four to six weeks. No events have occurred since his most recent review
